title: rootless container and pasta network performance is very bad with podman 5.8
body:
### Issue Description

Imn using the docker.io/registry:3 inside a rootless container, once with a port-publish on 443 and once with `--net=host`
Then another machine on the network tries to pull an image ~2GB in size
Both are running podman 5.8.0

For tests, i was using skopeo like this on the remote machine: (p14s-t is the one running the registry:3)
`time skopeo copy docker://p14s-t.sws.internal/test/bootc:43 dir:/tmp/test/`

First test/ethernet machine1:
Getting image source signatures
Copying blob 13348f75ec94 done   | 
Copying blob 96029e6c67cc done   | 
Copying config b632f19a62 done   | 
Writing manifest to image destination

real    2m32.450s
user    0m32.929s
sys     0m4.523s

Test2 WLAN machine2
real    1m12.794s
user    0m15.085s
sys     0m4.212s

Switching on the registry-server to `--net=host` i get

machine1/eth
real    0m17.151s
user    0m16.908s
sys     0m1.724s

machine2/wlan
real    0m18.320s
user    0m14.795s
sys     0m4.138s

Then i tried again after a reboot of the registry-server:
This time, machine1 was way quicker than before:

machine1/eth
real    1m17.440s
user    0m23.466s
sys     0m3.181s

I was using these machines and the registry before, end of last year with podman 5.7 
I know that pasta is slower than host-networking, but that much?
And, moreover, why the huge difference in speed after a reboot?

My previous tests (q4/2025) had been much better/more performant than this.

This smells like a regression somehow?

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
see above

### Describe the results you received

- expected time difference between pasta and `net=host`
- the slowdown with pasta is really too much to be practical, esp. with large images served by the registry

### Describe the results you expected

- pasta should be quicker
- the transfer-time results should be more or less consistent, not depending on reboots (for half the time in transfer)

So i might think, according to this testing, we still have a big problem buried in pasta somewhere.

(all the tests have been performed when both systems were idle, just the registry:3 running on p14s-t (amd7840u, nvme drives, should be beefy enough for serving content...)

> as you can see above, when testing with pasta 202509, i sometimes get the speed similar to `net=host` - this is what i wouldn't expect (but welcome) because the docs say we have a speed penalty with pasta

It's actually expected. The throughput penalty would only apply to the *maximum* throughput given by the computational and data copying/caching overhead, without other substantial bottlenecks. If you have a 1000BASE-T switch in between, that will be the bottleneck, not pasta (at least not on any post-modern x86 machine or equivalent).

We're generally talking about [these numbers](https://passt.top/passt/about/#performance_1), which only become relevant for local transfers or > 25 (perhaps 40) Gbps links.

> So i might think, according to this testing, we still have a big problem buried in pasta somewhere.

Right, I think so too.

Two more things that might help with debugging this:

- what is the round-trip time (RTT) between client and server? You can check that with a ping from client to server (or the other way around). I'm asking because we recently merged [some fixes](https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20251208072024.3884137-1-sbrivio@redhat.com/) for throughput issues we observed with non-local (but somewhat high throughput) transfers. The new mechanism to calculate the container/guest-side available window is rather adaptive and perhaps I didn't consider some corner case.

- I wonder if the application inside the container, or somehow the way we aggregate TCP segments in bigger chunks with pasta (by defaulting to the maximum possible MTU and hence MSS inside the container) doesn't play along with some of the links. Could you try starting the container with `podman run --net=pasta:--mtu,1500 ...` and see what happens?

> the registry:3 running on p14s-t (amd7840u, nvme drives, should be beefy enough for serving content...)

...yeah, absolutely.

@slartibart70, @huangyum prepared a draft patch based on a different approach to maximise the usage of the outbound sending buffer available and in turn trigger TCP buffer auto-tuning more transparently. It works pretty well in our tests so far. Would you mind giving it a try as well?

It's pretty easy, it should be a matter of seconds, as pasta (passt) doesn't have dependencies (other than a C library) and a local build can be uninstalled cleanly after the test. The patch is at https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20260320103214.99320-1-yuhuang@redhat.com/ and you can try it out with:

    git clone git://passt.top/passt && cd passt && b4 shazam https://archives.passt.top/passt-dev/20260320103214.99320-1-yuhuang@redhat.com/ && make && sudo make install

...or  `make install` as root if you don't use sudo. That can be reverted cleanly with `make uninstall` afterwards, and Podman will by default pick up the local test build.
